one_line_takeaway: ShieldedCode trains a hierarchical-attention, contrastively-regularized CodeLlama-34B to generate and rank VMP-protected code, genuinely beating GPT-4o on VMP code generation, but its headline binary-similarity-detection superiority over jTrans is drawn from one favorable table cell and is contradicted by the paper's own full result table.
key_finding: The clean, verifiable win is code generation (Table 1): ShieldedCode beats GPT-4o at every protection level, both Pass@1 (L0 26.95 vs 22.58, L1 18.47 vs 17.43, L2 19.23 vs 15.26, L3 14.71 vs 11.89) and Pass@10 (L0 35.68 vs 31.47, L1 27.94 vs 25.18, L2 29.71 vs 22.36, L3 22.83 vs 18.99), and clears all open-source baselines by wide margins. The binary-similarity-detection story (Table 2) is weaker than the abstract implies: the abstract's headline ('+15.5pp Recall@1 over jTrans Linear Probe,' 0.488 vs 0.333) cites only the single most favorable cell, O0+L1. Across all six O+L settings, ShieldedCode's Recall@1 (0.488, 0.306, 0.309, 0.272, 0.344, 0.469; mean ~0.365) trails jTrans (Linear Probe) on 5 of 6 settings (0.333, 0.573, 0.715, 0.404, 0.608, 0.601; mean ~0.539) by large margins, and its own mean is also below jTrans (Zero Shot)'s mean (~0.414) -- directly contradicting the paper's own in-text claim (Sec 4.4) of 'surpassing jTrans (Zero Shot) by 20.4% in averaged Recall@1.' Recomputing the table's own numbers does not support that averaged-superiority sentence. PEO (protection-strength ranking) and the reverse-engineering-resistance study (Table 5: ShieldedCode-protected code took 14.7±5.3 hours to reverse with a 17% success rate vs VMProtect 3.7's 3.4±1.2 hours/67% and Themida 3.1's 3.9±1.5 hours/58%; 0% pattern-matching success and 3% angr symbolic-execution completion vs VMProtect's 31%) are reported only as the paper's own claims from a small (12+3 participant) user study, not independently reproduced here.
core_contribution: ShieldedCode is a protection-aware representation-learning framework for virtual-machine-protected (VMP) code. It continually pre-trains CodeLlama-34B on a large paired corpus of source code and normalized VM implementations (produced via a commercial VMP tool + disassembler + canonicalization), using a hierarchical attention mask over three dependency levels (intra-instruction, preceding-instruction, inter-instruction) plus three joint objectives: standard language modeling, functionality-aware contrastive learning (FCL, pulls same-function embeddings together across protection levels with exponential proximity weighting), and protection-aware contrastive learning (PCL, a soft-margin loss that spreads embeddings apart proportionally to protection-level distance). A separate Protection Effectiveness Optimization (PEO) task with hard-negative mining is trained to rank which heavily-protected VM variant corresponds to a given lightly-protected query. Training is two-stage: continual pre-training (LM+FCL+PCL and PEO jointly) followed by fine-tuning (LM+FCL+PCL only) for either VM code generation or binary similarity representation learning.
threat_model: Defensive/dual-use framing: attackers use reverse engineering (static analysis, ML-based binary similarity detection, neural decompilation, automated deobfuscation/pattern-matching, symbolic execution) to break VMP-based intellectual-property/software protection and recover source-level semantics from obfuscated binaries. The paper positions its model as a tool for defenders to (a) generate stronger/more polymorphic VM protections and (b) evaluate protection strength by ranking VM variants (PEO), i.e. it is protection-generation and protection-assessment tooling, not an attack tool per se, though the same binary-similarity representation it trains is directly the capability attackers use for BCSD-based reverse engineering.
limitations: Headline binary-similarity claim ('+15.5pp / +20.4% over jTrans') is not representative of the full result table: ShieldedCode wins only 1 of 6 optimization/protection settings against jTrans (Linear Probe), and its own mean Recall@1 across settings appears below both jTrans variants when the table's own numbers are averaged -- an apparent inconsistency between the paper's narrative and its own Table 2 data. Ablation baselines ContraBin, CLAP, and CEBin in Table 4 are explicitly stated by the authors to be 'estimated based on their architectural capabilities' rather than measured -- these are not empirical results despite being presented in a results table alongside measured numbers. The VMP tool used to generate the training/eval corpus is an unnamed 'commercial VMP tool,' and the PEO pre-training corpus is cited as 'VirtuCorp 3M (Wang et al., 2022)' but the paper's own reference list has no dataset paper by that name under Wang 2022 (the 2022 Wang citation in the references is the jTrans paper) -- a reproducibility/citation-tracing concern. The manual reverse-engineering user study (12 students + 3 experts, 20 functions, fixed 30-45 min budget) is small and self-administered by the authors; no independent replication is presented. No public release of code, model weights, or the constructed VMP corpus is mentioned in the reproducibility statement beyond 'built on PyTorch and standard open-source libraries.'

datasets_used: VirtuCorp 3M (Wang et al., 2022, as cited) -- used for PEO pre-training
2.5M sampled source-to-VMP pairs (850M tokens, ~7GB) for VMCG fine-tuning
HumanEval compile (1000+ C functions, O0-O3, virtualized) -- code generation test set
BinaryCorp-VirtualAssembly (O0-O2 x L1/L3) -- binary similarity detection test set
Six real-world open-source projects held out from training: Binutils, Curl, ImageMagick, SQLite, OpenSSL, Putty -- PEO evaluation
quantitative_results: HumanEval compile Pass@1, ShieldedCode vs GPT-4o: L0 26.95 vs 22.58, L1 18.47 vs 17.43, L2 19.23 vs 15.26, L3 14.71 vs 11.89
HumanEval compile Pass@10, ShieldedCode vs GPT-4o: L0 35.68 vs 31.47, L1 27.94 vs 25.18, L2 29.71 vs 22.36, L3 22.83 vs 18.99
BinaryCorp-VirtualAssembly Recall@1, ShieldedCode: O0+L1 0.488, O1+L1 0.306, O2+L1 0.309, O0+L3 0.272, O1+L3 0.344, O2+L3 0.469
BinaryCorp-VirtualAssembly Recall@1, jTrans (Linear Probe): O0+L1 0.333, O1+L1 0.573, O2+L1 0.715, O0+L3 0.404, O1+L3 0.608, O2+L3 0.601

Averaging Table 2's own six Recall@1 columns: ShieldedCode mean ~0.365 vs jTrans Zero-Shot mean ~0.414 vs jTrans Linear-Probe mean ~0.539 -- ShieldedCode's mean is below both, contradicting the paper's text claim of a 20.4% average improvement over jTrans (Zero Shot)

Manual reverse-engineering study (Table 5): Unprotected 12.3±4.1 min / 100% success / 9.2/10 confidence; VMProtect 3.7 3.4±1.2 hr / 67% / 5.8/10; Themida 3.1 3.9±1.5 hr / 58% / 5.3/10; ShieldedCode 14.7±5.3 hr / 17% success / 2.1/10 confidence

Automated attacks: pattern-matching 0% success against ShieldedCode vs 43-61% against traditional VMP; angr symbolic execution 3% completion for ShieldedCode vs 31% for VMProtect, with 1,843x path explosion vs 127x for VMProtect

Ablation (Table 4, VMP corpus, Pass@1 averaged pattern): xVMP 16.82(L1), ShieldedCode-CL-PG (LM-only) 18.95(L1), ShieldedCode-PG (adds contrastive) raises overall Pass@1 from 21.86% to (full model adds polymorphic generation) 25.17%, Pass@10 from 35.25% to 38.30%, per paper text (note: ContraBin/CLAP/CEBin rows in same table are author-estimated, not measured)
baselines_compared: Code generation: CodeLlama, StarCoder2-7B, DeepSeekCoder-7B, Qwen-2.5-Coder-7B, Meta LLMCompiler-7B, GPT-3.5-Turbo, GPT-4o
Binary similarity detection: Gemini, GNN-based methods, OrderMatters, GraphEmb, SAFE, Asm2Vec, PalmTree, Trex, jTrans (Zero Shot), jTrans (Linear Probe), DeGPT, BinDiff, CodeBERT-Binary
Long-input handling: Granite 3B Code Base 128K (with and without ShieldedCode's approach applied)
Ablation/VMCG baselines: xVMP (reproduction), ContraBin, CLAP, CEBin (latter three author-estimated, not measured)
Reverse-engineering resistance: VMProtect 3.7, Themida 3.1 (commercial VMP tools), unprotected code
